headline: Elena Verna on Lovable's growth playbook: stop optimizing, start giving the product away
digest_md:
Elena Verna — head of growth at Lovable, ex-Miro, Dropbox, SurveyMonkey, Netlify, Amplitude — argues that the growth playbook she spent 15-20 years building only partly applies in this market. *'Only 30 to 40% of what I've learned in the last 15 to 20 years of being in growth transfers here,'* she says. Her old job was 5% innovation, 95% optimization. At Lovable it's the inverse.

The central instinct her team is fighting: *protectiveness*. Traditional growth PMs guard revenue, gate trials, ration access, A/B test the funnel. Verna's team does the opposite — give the product away aggressively, including handing free credits to users running hackathons or building agents that distribute Lovable for them. *'Why would we prevent a person who wants to do all of the marketing and activating for us from using us?'*

She is explicit that the team does **not** optimize for revenue right now: *'we don't optimize for revenue at all... we have a lot of discussions about how can we give more products away.'* The North Star is engagement retention — the leading indicator of paid retention later. Subscriber-retention dashboards come second.

On what actually moves the needle: building in public (founder-led and employee socials), shipping things people want to talk about, and standing up new growth loops faster than competitors. The old optimization muscle — tweaking activation drop-offs by 3% — is the wrong work for a market this perishable. Reinvention beats refinement.

The quality bar holds it all together: the only way to create a word-of-mouth loop is to *'just blow their socks off.'* No volume of distribution rescues a forgettable product.
takeaways: If you're a growth PM mostly doing funnel A/B tests, your week is probably misallocated. In a fast-moving market, optimization is 5% of the value; new loops are 95%.
Stop guarding the free tier. Identify the users who would evangelize you if you handed them more — give it to them this week, no approval chain.
Don't optimize for revenue while you're still proving the product. Pick engagement retention as your weekly metric until usage growth is clearly compounding.
Building in public — founder and employee socials — is a growth surface, not a marketing tactic. If you're a PM, lobby for it on your team this week.

questions.scenario_md:
You're a growth PM at a fast-moving AI product company. Looking at your last quarter: you ran 14 A/B tests on the signup flow, lifted activation 3.2%, and shipped a referral copy variation. You want to bring this list to your skip-level next week as evidence of strong execution.

Per Elena Verna's framing of growth in this market, the most accurate read on your quarter is —

options.text: Strong execution — incremental funnel wins compound and demonstrate growth-PM discipline.
Mostly optimization work; in a perishable market your week should be inverted toward inventing new loops.
Solid foundation — once activation stabilizes you can layer in retention experiments.
Misallocated — you should have spent the quarter rewriting the pricing page to reduce friction.

questions.explanation_md: Verna's framing inverts the old growth-PM ratio: in a fast-moving market she runs 95% innovation and 5% optimization, because optimizations are not worth the time when the market is perishable. A quarter of A/B-tested funnel tweaks is the wrong-shape week, not a strong one.
questions.pm_takeaway: This week, kill one optimization on your roadmap and replace it with one new growth loop you have not tried before — even a rough one shipped is the right work.

citation.title: Elena Verna 4.0
citation.quote_excerpt: I usually spend maybe 5% innovating on growth in my previous roles, right now, I'm spending 95% innovating on growth, and only 5% on optimization.

questions.scenario_md:
A power user emails: she's running an internal hackathon for 80 engineers at a mid-size company on your AI tool and asks for a bunch of free credits to hand out. Your finance partner has flagged margin concerns this quarter. You're the PM the email lands on. Four moves on the table:

1. Approve the credits same-day, no friction.
2. Offer a 30% discount code instead of free credits.
3. Loop in finance and sales for an approval review.
4. Politely decline; suggest she try the standard free tier first.

Per Verna's playbook, the move you make is —

options.text: Option 3 — finance/sales sign-off ensures margin discipline.
Option 4 — preserve the paywall; the standard free tier is the policy.
Option 2 — discount preserves some revenue while still rewarding the request.
Option 1 — approve same-day; she is doing the marketing and activation for you.

questions.explanation_md: Verna's view is that someone running a hackathon is doing your marketing and activation for you — the cost of the credits is dwarfed by the cost of friction in front of an evangelist. Discounts and approval chains both leak the moment.
questions.pm_takeaway: Tomorrow, find one inbound user who is already evangelizing your product and unblock them with no approval chain — credits, access, whatever they asked for.

citation.title: Elena Verna 4.0
citation.quote_excerpt: Why would we prevent a person who wants to do all of the marketing and activating for us from using us? We're like, take it, how much do you need?

questions.scenario_md:
Your AI product is six months post-launch with strong top-of-funnel growth. Your VP of Growth proposes the team's Q3 North Star be 'paid subscriber retention rate,' arguing investors will care. You're the senior PM owning the metrics review and need to take a position before Friday.

Per Verna's framing, the missing step before you adopt that North Star is —

options.text: Confirm engagement retention is the leading indicator you track first; paid retention can lag once usage compounds.
Run a benchmark study on B2B SaaS retention curves to set the target.
Add NDR alongside subscriber retention so investor-facing numbers look complete.
Tighten the paywall to reduce churn before you commit to a retention number.

questions.explanation_md: Verna says engagement retention is the leading indicator for paid retention, and Lovable explicitly does not optimize for revenue right now — usage first, monetization later. Picking paid retention as the North Star inverts that order.
questions.pm_takeaway: Before Friday, draft a one-page case for engagement retention as the team's leading metric, with paid retention as the lagging check — and put it in your VP's hands.

citation.title: Elena Verna 4.0
citation.quote_excerpt: we're honestly more focused right now on engagement retention than even paid retention because our North Star is just to get as much usage as possible, and we will fix and tune our monetization model afterwards.

questions.scenario_md:
You're a PM on a B2B AI feature team. Marketing owns the company blog. You ship things every week, but no one outside the team sees them — your demos sit in internal Slack. Your manager asks how you'd contribute to top-of-funnel growth this quarter without adding paid acquisition spend.

The response that best translates Verna's 'building in public' lever to your role this week is —

options.text: Pitch marketing on a quarterly campaign for your feature with vetted messaging and approved screenshots.
Start posting raw weekly demos and behind-the-scenes notes from your own account; rally two engineers to do the same.
Run a paid LinkedIn pilot to test which feature messages convert.
Wait for a polished launch moment so the first public post lands well.

questions.explanation_md: Verna names building in public — coupled with employee and founder-led socials — as one of the few things actually moving the needle. The translation for a PM is direct, raw, recurring posts from individual accounts, not a polished marketing campaign.
questions.pm_takeaway: This week, post one short demo or build-log of something you shipped from your own account, and ask one teammate to do the same.

citation.title: Elena Verna 4.0
citation.quote_excerpt: One of our biggest strategy is building in public, and it's coupled with employee socials, founder-led socials. And another one is giving your product away a lot, this is part of our growth secret sauce.

questions.scenario_md: You inherit a referral program with a 0.4% activation-to-referral rate. The growth analyst proposes A/B testing the share-modal copy and bumping the reward from $5 to $10. You have one week before the next sprint planning. Per Verna's view of how word-of-mouth is actually generated, the highest-leverage move is —

options.text: Investigate which moments in the product genuinely surprise users and double down on making one of them sharper.
Ship the $10 reward and the new copy; small lifts compound.
Replace the modal with a one-tap social-share button to reduce friction.
Pause the program; reroute the spend to paid acquisition until activation lifts.

questions.explanation_md: Verna's framing is that the only way to create a word-of-mouth loop is to make the product genuinely impressive — to 'blow their socks off.' Reward and copy tweaks optimize a referral mechanic that fires only when the underlying product moment is share-worthy.
questions.pm_takeaway: This week, identify the single user moment that surprises people most in your product, and put one engineer-day into making it sharper — before touching any referral mechanic.

citation.title: Elena Verna 4.0
citation.quote_excerpt: The only way to create a word of mouth loop is just to blow their socks off.
